tests.reason: A 12.55 Msun MS star would be a late-B type star with L ~ 20,000 Lsun and Teff ~ 22,000 K, outshining the 30 Lsun K-giant primary by a factor of ~700.  The SED would be completely dominated by a hot blue source.  The observed K-giant SED categorically excludes this scenario.
M2 = 12.55 Msun exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit (1.44 Msun) by a factor of 9.  No known single WD can reach this mass.
M2 = 12.55 Msun exceeds the TOV limit (2.3 Msun) by a factor of 5.  Even the most generous NS EOS cannot accommodate this mass.  Even at M1 = 0.5 Msun, M2 remains above 10 Msun.

Stability requires P_inner < 73.4 d.  Two ~6.3 Msun MS stars would each be mid-B type stars with combined L ~ 5,000 Lsun, easily detectable against the 30 Lsun K giant.  Moreover, the AstroSpectroSB1 solution requires a single Keplerian orbit — a triple would produce additional RV complexity not seen in the data.

A 12.55 Msun stripped He star or Wolf-Rayet star would have L > 30,000 Lsun and Teff > 30,000 K, completely dominating the SED.  The source would appear as a bright blue object, inconsistent with the observed K-giant spectrum.

The AstroSpectroSB1 solution has significance 39.9σ (8× the threshold of 5).  Critically, this is a COMBINED astrometric + spectroscopic solution: Gaia independently detects the orbital signal in BOTH photo-centre motion AND radial velocities.  This cross-confirmation essentially eliminates pure astrometric artefacts.  RUWE = 9.2 is extreme, consistent with binary photo-centre motion.  EN_sig = 1762 is very high.  GoF = 3.07 is acceptable.  The parallax has only 15.5% relative error (much better than typical Orbital solutions).

The AstroSpectroSB1 solution represents a coherent Keplerian signal at 40σ over 1352 d.  The eccentricity (e = 0.532) is physical.  At G = 12.3 (bright), source confusion is minimal.  The measured radial velocity (RV = -5.04 ± 2.08 km/s) provides an independent kinematic confirmation of bound-system membership.  The SB1 component of the solution directly measures RV variations consistent with the orbital period, ruling out chance alignment.

conclusion: VERY STRONG BH CANDIDATE: All seven alternative scenarios are excluded.  The AstroSpectroSB1 solution provides independent astrometric and spectroscopic confirmation of the orbital signal.  At 39.8σ significance with M2 = 12.55 Msun, this is a robust stellar-mass BH candidate comparable to the confirmed Gaia BH1 and BH2 systems.
